keyPoints: No published six-week slow-mode RCT measuring loneliness and perceived conversational depth.
Latency effects as social cues vary by context and user characteristics.
Loneliness reductions mediated by feeling heard and perceived emotional support.
Perceived conversational depth driven by social presence and warmth, not latency alone.
Multi-arm design needed to disentangle latency, prompts, and social-cue effects.
Longitudinal mediation analyses with week-by-week trajectories and preregistered hypotheses.
Pilot testing latency window and prompts before full six-week trial.
Adaptive or personalized latency policies to account for user differences.
